questions.question: Explain the DDA algorithm for line drawing. What are its advantages and disadvantages compared to Bresenham's algorithm?
keyPoints: DDA computes intermediate points using incremental calculation based on slope
Uses floating-point arithmetic to calculate x = x + 1/m and y = y + m
Advantage: Simple to understand and implement
Disadvantage: Uses floating-point operations (slower) and accumulates rounding errors
Bresenham's uses only integer arithmetic and is more efficient

questions.question: What is the scan-line polygon fill algorithm? Explain how it handles concave polygons.
keyPoints: Identifies scan lines that intersect the polygon
For each scan line, finds intersection points with polygon edges
Sorts intersections in pairs from left to right
Fills pixels between each pair
For concave polygons, may generate multiple intersection pairs per scan line
Requires edge table and active edge list for efficiency

questions.question: Write and explain the decision logic for Bresenham's line algorithm when drawing lines with slope between 0 and 1.
keyPoints: Initialize P₀ = 2Δy - Δx where Δy = y1-y0, Δx = x1-x0
At each step, if Pk < 0: select pixel (xk+1, yk), update Pk+1 = Pk + 2Δy
If Pk ≥ 0: select pixel (xk+1, yk+1), update Pk+1 = Pk + 2Δy - 2Δx
Repeat until endpoint is reached
This logic ensures the closest pixel to the true line is selected

questions.question: Explain the difference between boundary fill and flood fill algorithms. Under what circumstances would you use each?
keyPoints: Boundary fill: fills region bounded by a specific color border
Flood fill: replaces all connected pixels of target color with new color
Boundary fill used for: drawings with defined outlines, cartoons, clip art
Flood fill used for: image editing, paint applications, region recoloring
Flood fill requires a seed point; boundary fill does not

questions.question: What is aliasing in computer graphics? Explain any one anti-aliasing technique used to reduce aliasing effects.
keyPoints: Aliasing: stair-step effect on diagonal lines due to discrete pixel representation
Anti-aliasing: technique to smooth jagged edges
Wu's Algorithm: uses intensity variation to create smooth lines
Pre-calculate pixel intensity based on distance from true line
Higher intensity pixels near the line, lower intensity away from line
Result: visually smoother appearance at cost of more computation

questions.question: Explain the Bresenham's circle algorithm. How does it determine the next pixel position?
keyPoints: Draws circle using only integer arithmetic
Initial decision parameter: P₀ = 1 - r for radius r
At each step, if Pk < 0: plot (xk+1, yk), Pk+1 = Pk + 2xk+1 + 1
If Pk ≥ 0: plot (xk+1, yk-1), Pk+1 = Pk + 2xk+1 + 1 - 2yk-1
Algorithm exploits 8-way symmetry to draw complete circle
More efficient than trigonometric approaches

questions.question: Write a detailed algorithm for the DDA line drawing method. Explain how it handles: (a) lines with slope magnitude less than 1, and (b) lines with slope magnitude greater than 1. Also discuss why DDA may produce different results for lines drawn in opposite directions.
keyPoints: Initialize: x = x1, y = y1, steps = max(|dx|, |dy|)
Calculate: xin = dx/steps, yin = dy/steps
Plot first point, then iterate: x = xxin, y = y + yin, plot round(x), round(y)
For |m| < 1: step in x direction, calculate y incrementally
For |m| > 1: swap roles, step in y direction, calculate x incrementally
Opposite direction drawing: rounding errors accumulate differently
This can cause lines drawn (x1,y1)→(x2,y2) and (x2,y2)→(x1,y1) to differ
Algorithm is simple but slower than Bresenham due to floating-point operations

questions.question: Compare and contrast the DDA algorithm and Bresenham's line algorithm. Include in your answer: (i) the mathematical basis of each algorithm, (ii) computational complexity, (iii) accuracy considerations, and (iv) practical applications.
keyPoints: DDA: uses slope to incrementally compute coordinates, requires floating-point arithmetic
Bresenham: uses decision variable based on line equation, uses only integer operations
DDA time: O(n) with floating-point ops; Bresenham: O(n) with integer ops (faster in practice)
DDA accumulates rounding errors; Bresenham is exact for pixel positions
Bresenham is industry standard for line drawing due to efficiency
DDA used in educational contexts and simple implementations
Both produce same pixel sets for same direction; differ for opposite directions
Bresenham extends to circles, ellipses with same principles

questions.question: Describe the scan-line fill algorithm for polygon filling. Explain the data structures required (edge table and active edge list) and how the algorithm processes each scan line to fill the polygon correctly.
keyPoints: Preprocessing: build edge table sorted by y-coordinate of lower endpoint
For each scan line: create active edge list from edges crossing that line
Active edge list stores: x-intersection, inverse slope (1/m), and edge endpoint
Sort active edge list by x-coordinate of intersection
Process intersections in pairs: fill between pairs on current scan line
Update active edge list: remove edges ending at current line, update x for others
Repeat for each scan line from bottom to top of polygon
Handles both convex and concave polygons correctly
Complexity: O(n + m) where n is edges, m is scan lines

questions.question: Explain the concept of raster graphics and the need for scan conversion algorithms. Discuss how line drawing algorithms like Bresenham's ensure that pixels selected are the closest possible to the true line geometry.
keyPoints: Raster graphics: discrete pixel grid representing images
Scan conversion: process of converting geometric primitives to pixel representation
Bresenham's algorithm: at each step, chooses between two candidate pixels
Decision based on comparing line's position to midpoint between candidates
Decision parameter P tracks relative position of line from midpoint
Pk < 0: line closer to lower pixel; Pk ≥ 0: line closer to upper pixel
This ensures optimal pixel selection minimizing error
Algorithm equivalent to minimizing maximum deviation from true line
Works for all slopes by handling octants separately
Proves mathematically that selected pixels form closest approximation
